title: The July Wage Increase You Haven't Modelled Yet
linkedin:
Most business owners know the July award wage increase is coming. Very few have calculated what it actually costs their specific payroll before the first pay run of FY27 arrives.

The Fair Work Commission sets a new base rate each June for every covered classification. The part less often worked through is how the increase compounds through the rest of the payroll calculation.

Superannuation is calculated on ordinary time earnings. When the base rate rises, the employer super obligation rises proportionally for every affected employee. The Q1 contribution does not fall due until late October, but the liability accrues from the first July pay period.

Annual leave loading of 17.5 per cent is calculated on the base ordinary time rate. For businesses where staff concentrate leave in July and August after the EOFY push, a higher base rate amplifies payroll outflows across those specific months beyond what the headline percentage implies.

Penalty rates, casual loadings, and overtime all derive from the ordinary time rate. Every hour worked under a loading carries the base rate increase through it, not just standard hours. For businesses with complex shift patterns, the effective weekly cost increase is consistently higher than the headline percentage alone.

July also carries concentrated pressure from other directions. Revenue is rebuilding from its post-EOFY transition. The Q4 FY26 super guarantee falls due on 28 July for businesses that have not pre-paid.

The June window is there to run the arithmetic before the first payroll arrives, not after.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance

The part that catches businesses out is how the increase compounds. The base rate flows through superannuation, annual leave loading, and every penalty and casual loading on top. The real weekly cost is almost always higher than what the headline percentage implies when applied to ordinary wages alone.

Have you calculated what the first payroll of FY27 will actually cost for your team?

google:
If your business employs award-covered staff, the Fair Work Commission's annual wage decision takes effect from the first full pay period after 1 July. The new rates apply across hospitality, childcare, aged care, allied health, retail, construction, and most other sectors with significant award coverage.

Most business owners are aware the increase is coming. What is worth calculating now, before the first payroll of FY27 runs, is the real cost for your specific roster.

The headline percentage understates it for most businesses. Superannuation is calculated on ordinary time earnings, so the employer obligation rises proportionally. Annual leave loading of 17.5 per cent applies to the new base rate, which matters for July and August when teams often concentrate leave after the EOFY push. Penalty rates, casual loadings, and weekend penalties all derive from the base rate, so every hour worked under a loading carries the increase through it.

July carries additional pressure from other directions. Revenue is typically rebuilding from its post-EOFY transition. The Q4 FY26 superannuation guarantee falls due on 28 July for businesses that have not pre-paid. Higher payroll costs run from week one of the new financial year.

The full breakdown, including how to calculate the real weekly cost for your specific roster and whether a pricing review makes sense before the new year begins, is on the ProfitPulse blog:
https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-july-wage-increase-you-haven-t-modelled-yet
